his most pleasant of tasks, the acknowledging of gratitude to those
whose efforts and encouragement have facilitated a writer's work, must
in this case be tinged with a note of sorrow, since r. endrick robel,
under whose guidance this study was begun, did not live to see it to
completion as an academic dissertation. r. robel was my teacher in
optic, but the present work owes quite as much to his personal in-
terest and encouragement. fter his most unepected death, rs. ro-
bel was her usual gracious self in allowing me continued access to her
husband's library.
t   considerable   inconvenience   to   himself,   r. . c. ilson
voluntarily assumed the responsibility of seeing the study through as
a dissertation. o him  epress my thanks.
 most etraordinary note of thanks is due to the atristische om-
mission, and to its personnel in ünster. nder its auspices the cost of
publication has been underwritten, and its most careful attention at
every stage has made this book possible. t is an especially great honor
for r. artin rause to have agreed to supply an introduction to the
linguistic aspects of the tet, thus greatly amplifying the value of the
publication.
r. . hlers and . . osenbaum of ünster have made an es-
sential contribution to the entire study by their eacting and tireless
work in the checking of references to writers ancient and modern, and
their reworking of the bibliography.  owe them my heartfelt thanks.
nd certainly not least,  am grateful for the attention and good ad-
vice of r. urt land.
ource material for this study has not always been readily available.
n this regard  epress my gratitude for the assistance of iss lara
rown and her staff at the oint niversities ibrary, iss ary ird
of the ardy ibrary of tlantic hristian ollege, and r. . . s-
burn of outheastern aptist heological eminary. he aculty el-

financial assistance during the preparation of the manuscript.
he whole of the work reflects the instruction and encouragement
over many years of r. . . riggs, and the concerned interest of my

parents. ut of those to whom  epress my thanks, none would e-
pect least credit while deserving most than my wife arol, of whom
the words of irach — () must have been written.
hese sources of help have greatly assisted this work, but the final
responsibility for error rests with the author.

umbered as the fourth writing contained in ode  of the optic
nostic documents recovered from ag-ammadi  ,   he   ypostasis
of the rchons was first made available to the scholarly world by a-
hor abib's publication of a volume of photographic reproductions of
the pages of a portion of the code  . ince this represents the most
convenient access to the ag-ammadi   treatises contained therein,
the pagination of abib's book has been followed here. owever, the
edition here presented has made use of photographs of the manuscript,
graciously loaned by r. artin rause of ünster.
 short document of  lines, he ypostasis of the rchons has
thus far not received a great deal of attention. enri-harles uech
and ean oresse have published surveys of the entire ag-ammadi
discovery,   which   included a   few pages relative   to   this   document  .
ther than a few fragments offered in translation as illustrative mate-
rial to other studies  , the only rendering of the treatise into a modern
language is the erman translation of ans-artin chenke  .

   ullard, ichoos

ince he ypostasis of the rchons, like most nostic literature, is
of primary interest to the student, and is not likely to earn a place on
prestigious   library   shelves   nestling   the   literary   classics   of   human
history, the translation is accordingly prepared with the scholar in
mind. t the epense of literary finesse and nicer turns of nglish
idiom, the translator is presenting a very literal version, being careful
to maintain the integrity of the original lineation.
he commentary is keyed to the tet by page and line. he division
of the commentary into sections with titles corresponds to nothing in
the tet itself, but is a device to facilitate reference and understanding.
he scope of the commentary embraces problems of content and of
language.   he   disadvantages   of   combining   linguistic   and   palaeo-
graphical commentary with remarks on the thought content of the
document will be evident, but since in many places the two cannot be
separated, one set of notes only is provided. ommentaries by their
very nature do not lend themselves to graceful presentation, and with
this thought in mind, the writer offers what he considers the most prac-
tical format for the study.
he   purpose   of   these   notes is to   elucidate   the   meaning   of   the
writing in its immediate contet. haustive lists of parallel references
in related literature are not provided, nor is every historical and theo-
logical problem encountered thoroughly investigated. he intent has
rather been to demonstrate the relations of this document, and sug-
gest the areas of research that may be brought to bear upon it in
studies of less comprehensive scope but of greater depth.
he   most   closely   related   literature   is,   of   course,   other   nostic
writings which have been published, as well as the accounts of the
nostic sectarians given in the works of the great heresiologists of the
early   hurch. andean and anichean literature has been utilized
here only where it offered some real elucidation of the tet at hand.
he problem posed by the relation of these optic nostic writings to
the development of andean and anichean religion is a large one,
and the place for this study is in concentrated investigations directed
precisely at that area. iscussion of the matter within the organization
of a commentary would make for hopeless incoherence, nor would it
always be directly relevant to the present tet.
omething of the same situation holds in regard to the relations to
ewish literature, in particular, the literature of later ewish mysticism.
cores of striking parallels can be adduced, but since they throw no
light on the understanding of this document in its own historical con-
tet, they go uncited. he traditions of the rabbinic literature, how-
ever, are more relevant, since much of the tradition finding epression
in the rabbinic writings is roughly contemporary with the gyptian
gnostics. se has therefore been made of these sources, although the

writer must confess that the effective use of rabbinic material is ham-
pered by his own lack of thorough acquaintance with its full range,
and in some cases, by the inaccessibility of the material itself.
atters pertaining to the palaeographic hand and the date of the
manuscript have been thoroughly discussed elsewhere, in treatments
of more familiar writings from ode , and need no repetition here®.
uffice it to say that the manuscript is from somewhere around 
. ., although the material contained in it, including parts of this
document, may be older, perhaps much older.
rom a literary point of view, he ypostasis of the rchons gives
the appearance of having been compiled from two or three sources,
with editorial epansion by a hristian nostic. ne of these sources
is purely narrative in form and cosmological in concern, essentially a
nostic interpretation of the first chapters of enesis, with no evi-
dence of hristian influence, save for an occasional gloss. he second
main source is an apocalyptic writing in the form of question and
answer. his section is concerned with eschatology and soteriology in a
way that the first is not. he beginning and conclusion to the document
are certainly from a hristian nostic, whose fondness for ohn and
phesians is evident.
he material has to do with the origin and nature of the archontic
powers peopling the heavens between arth and the gdoad, and how
the destiny of man is affected by these primeval happenings. t will be
noticed that of the various nostic sects described by the hurch
athers, the phite groups seem to have a closer relation to the ideas
of this treatise than do others, though on occasion notions distinctly
alentinian are evident.
he phite connections suggest a tradition of some age. he docu-
ment shows some signs of stemming from a transitional period in
nosticism, as it was coming out of a purely mythological stage and
was beginning to recast in a philosophical manner the substance of its
own traditions and of others not yet digested into its system. s the
writing progresses, it is evident that all its parts are not on the same
level of sophistication, and presumably, not of the same age.
